title: Comps: The Sector's Heartbeat Metric
quick_definition: Comparable store sales ("comps," same-store sales) measure growth at stores open past a threshold (commonly ~13 months) — stripping out new-store openings to reveal whether the existing business is actually growing. It is the retailer's heartbeat, and it decomposes into traffic and ticket: how many customers came, and what each spent.
explainer_covers: Why comps exists (★G318). A retailer can grow revenue forever by opening stores while every existing store quietly shrinks — comps is the metric that catches it. It separates demand growth from footprint growth, which is why it anchors the growth algorithm in Z4.1.

The decomposition is the diagnosis. Starbucks FY2025: North America comparable-store sales were down 2%, driven by transactions down 4% and average ticket up 2% — fewer customers, paying more. The two components tell different stories (traffic is demand/brand health; ticket is pricing/mix), and the same print with reversed components would mean the opposite thesis. This is ★G192/★G193's KPI-before-EPS discipline with the sector's own instrument.

Every filer defines its own comp base — inclusion thresholds, FX treatment, remodels, digital attribution all vary (Nike's Direct-store comps −1% under its definition) — so the footnote is part of the metric (deepened in Z4.2).

The metric travels. Restaurants and retail use comps directly; lodging runs the same logic as comparable-property RevPAR = occupancy × ADR (Z2.3) — occupancy is lodging's traffic, ADR its ticket. One decomposition family, three industries.

The collision, defused. These comps are a demand metric. ★G88's "comps" (comparable-companies analysis) and ★G89's "deal comps" are valuation methods — peer multiples and precedent transactions. A retail analyst genuinely uses both in one sentence ("run comps on the comps"); the formal disambiguation family lands here.
ER's contrast, fulfilled: this node plus Z3.2 is exactly the "same-store sales, traffic, and margin" the hub has named since M8.

title: Margin & Inventory: The Retail P&L's Two Pressure Points
quick_definition: A consumer company's health reads through two linked gauges: gross margin (what survives after the cost of the goods — the report card of brand strength, channel mix, and discipline) and inventory (the stock of goods whose turns, markdowns, and obsolescence drive that margin — and whose management is the sector's classic failure mode).
explainer_covers: Margin as a verdict, not a line. Nike FY2025: gross margin 42.7%, down 190 bps — the filing's own causes: discounts, channel mix, obsolescence reserves. Every driver is a decision or a demand fact; margin is where brand power (Z3.4), channel choice (Z2.2), and inventory discipline all get graded at once. Operating margin adds the cost of running the fleet (Starbucks: 7.9%, down 710 bps — the fixed-cost store base amplifying a comp decline). Amazon's honest footnote: it tells you itself that segment operating income is its meaningful lens, not gross margin — read companies in their own accounting dialect. (Gross margin is deliberately not minted — it's corpus-generic accounting; the retail inventory discipline is the sector's own science, and that's the global ★G319.)

Inventory — the sector's inventory science. Turns (how fast stock converts to sales), markdowns (the price you pay to move a buying mistake), obsolescence reserves (fashion ages; Nike holds $7.5B of inventory against exactly that clock), shrink. Stone's early-Amazon toy write-offs are the canonical cautionary tale: in retail, being wrong about demand doesn't just miss revenue — it sits in a warehouse depreciating.

The cash cycle — and the negative operating cycle. Cash conversion = days of inventory + receivables − payables. Scale retail's superpower (Stone, Ch. 2): turn inventory before paying suppliers and customers' cash funds the growth — the negative operating cycle that let Amazon expand on its vendors' float. The flywheel's balance-sheet trick.

Inventory as collateral (the credit hook). Retail's asset-based lending borrows against the inventory itself — ★G76's borrowing base with merchandise in the box; deepened with the credit reading in Z4.2.

Working-capital kinship. ★G31 is the deal-closing working-capital peg in PE; this node is the sector application of those same working-capital mechanics to retail inventory and the cash cycle — operating turns, markdowns, and float — not a separate concept family.

title: The Polarized Consumer: Trading Up, Trading Down & the Dying Middle
quick_definition: Discretionary spending doesn't cut evenly — consumers trade up in the few categories they care about (paying real premiums for "New Luxury" goods within middle-class reach) while trading down everywhere else to fund it. The result is the sector's demand-structure law: the premium and value poles grow, and the middle dies.
explainer_covers: Trading up (Silverstein & Fiske) — ★G320. Middle-market consumers pay 20–200% premiums in chosen categories — breaking the classical price-volume curve (high price and high volume). "New Luxury" comes in three types, folded here as orbit vocabulary: accessible superpremium (the $28 vodka), Old-Luxury brand extensions (the entry Mercedes), and masstige (prestige at a mass price point).

Rocketing and the funded trade-down. The same household buys the $3,000 golf clubs and shops at the discounter — selective intensity in 2–3 categories, ruthless value elsewhere. The trade-down is not weakness; it's the funding mechanism of the trade-up.

The benefit ladder (orbit). Winners ladder technical → functional → emotional benefits — the product has to actually be better and mean something. Emotional-only Old Luxury gets dethroned when the technical rungs rot (the book's Cadillac autopsy — a Z2.4 rhyme).

Death in the middle — the strategic law. Positioned between the poles = squeezed by both. This is the sector's map of who dies: the mid-tier department store, the unremarkable mid-price brand. For an investor, where a company sits on the polarization is often the thesis itself — premium and discount can both work; the middle is where retailers go to die.

The cycle interaction. Downturns accelerate polarization — the wallet shrinks, the rocketing gets more selective, the trade-down deepens (Z5.1's mood-ring read; Stephens's crisis-shopping psychology as the pandemic-era echo).
Why this global and not five. Masstige, rocketing, the benefit ladder, apostles — all real vocabulary, all folded into this one home rather than minted.

title: Brand: The Asset You Build in the Customer's Head
quick_definition: Brand equity is the economic asset a company builds in customers' heads — the accumulated meaning that lets a product command a premium, hold traffic, and survive a bad season. In a sector where the customer can always defer or substitute, brand is a core productive asset, and differentiation is the craft of building one that the competitive herd can't copy.
explainer_covers: Brand as an asset, not an ad (★G321). The sneaker costs dollars to make; the swoosh commands the price. Brand power's paper trail runs through the module's own gauges: pricing power → gross margin (Z3.2); loyalty → traffic (Z3.1); premium positioning → the trade-up (Z3.3). An intangible with a P&L signature.

Why differentiation decays (Moon's diagnosis). The herd instinct: shared metrics and positioning maps make competitors converge; the augmentation treadmill adds features until every offering blurs (heterogeneous homogeneity — dozens of SKUs, no real choice); customers respond with category blur — loyalty erodes to the category level. Being "better" on the shared scorecard is how brands become invisible.

The escape typology (Moon). Reverse brands subtract what the category over-added (Google's empty page, IKEA's absent services); breakaway brands reclassify the offering so the evaluation criteria change (Swatch: watch → fashion accessory; Cirque: circus → theater); hostile brands draw a line some customers won't cross and gain identity heat from the refusal (Red Bull, MINI). Difference, not incremental betterness, is what re-earns attention.

Brand and durable advantage — the line held. Brand can be one source of durable competitive advantage in consumer businesses — but ★G321 claims the asset (brand equity and differentiation), not the corpus-wide "moat" concept, which remains deliberately unhomed (`MIGRATION_DEFECTS` §G #16, OPEN). Tech's version of a related advantage question is often answered by network effects (★G298) — a cross-sector contrast worth one bullet, not a family that resolves moat.
Purpose, archetypes & apostles (orbit). Stephens's ten retail archetypes and purpose-as-positioning; Silverstein's apostles (the passionate few who seed demand) — the practitioner layer folded here.
The staples seam (stated). Brand arguably matters even more where purchases repeat weekly — when the Consumer Staples module is built, it back-links this global rather than re-homing it.
